name: Force 3-CTA Residency On The PTX 128x128 Hot Band

summary.name: Force 3-CTA Residency On The PTX 128x128 Hot Band
summary.hypothesis: The latest grouped_rows=8 regression makes the core plateau constraint explicit. The hot-band PTX kernel is still the dominant kernel at about 32.69 us, but it runs with 200 registers per thread, 22,016 B shared memory per block, only 2 resident CTAs per SM, and therefore only 8 active warps per SM or about 16.6% achieved warps. That is a structural latency-hiding wall, not a DRAM wall. The next move should therefore restore the exact 489574e PTX winner surface and then aggressively tighten the hot-band PTX branch around __launch_bounds__ and obvious live ranges so ptxas is forced to target 3-CTA residency. If that succeeds, the hot-band kernel can move from 8 toward 12 warps per SM without reopening the already-settled grouped_rows or export-order branches.
summary.expected_bottleneck: Register-limited occupancy and latency hiding in the 128x128 PTX hot-band kernel, not global bandwidth.
summary.risk: Moderate. Correctness risk is low because the output ownership model stays on the proven PTX winner surface, but performance risk is real because forcing a tighter launch budget can introduce spills or expose a different barrier mix.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime with a >0.15 ms improvement threshold over the current 24.517119 ms run
ranking_notes: This is the highest-upside bounded change still compatible with one round of node_c work.
It directly attacks the measured 200-reg / 2-CTA residency wall instead of chasing another sub-0.1 ms PTX ordering difference.
The user guidance now explicitly treats <0.1 ms as noise, so the ranking favors structural occupancy moves over exact restore loops.
summary.stop_condition: Stop this family quickly if launch__registers_per_thread stays above about 190 or launch__occupancy_limit_registers remains 2 with no material runtime gain; improvements smaller than 0.10 ms should be treated as flat noise, not a real win.

notes: Node C may now implement this one candidate.
